elements.sent-2411-001: Despite the dominant role of deep models in machine learning, limitations persist, including overconfident predictions, susceptibility to adversarial attacks, and underestimation of variability in predictions.
elements.sent-2411-002: The Bayesian paradigm provides a natural framework to overcome such issues and has become the gold standard for uncertainty estimation with deep models, also providing improved accuracy and a framework for tuning critical hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, exact Bayesian inference is challenging, typically involving variational algorithms that impose strong independence and distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, existing methods are sensitive to the architectural choice of the network.
elements.sent-2411-005: We address these issues by constructing a relaxed version of the standard feed-forward rectified neural network, and employing Polya-Gamma data augmentation tricks to render a conditionally linear and Gaussian model.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we use sparsity-promoting priors on the weights of the neural network for data-driven architectural design.
elements.sent-2411-007: To approximate the posterior, we derive a variational inference algorithm that avoids distributional assumptions and independence across layers and is a faster alternative to the usual Markov Chain Monte Carlo schemes.
elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: Bayesian neural networks, variational inference, uncertainty quantification, shrinkage priors.
elements.sent-2411-009: Neural networks (NNs) are effective deep models that play a dominant role in machine learning and have achieved remarkable success across various domains including medicine and biological sciences \cite{jumper2021highly, YU2021 , natural language processing \cite{mikolov2013, touvron2023llamaopenefficientfoundation , computer vision and image analysis \cite{dosovitskiy2021imageworth16x16words , data privacy and security \cite{yang2019 and beyond.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, modern machine learning applications often lack reliable, if not any, uncertainty estimates \cite{guo17, Gal2016Uncertainty, ashukha2020pitfalls .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Classical deep models are easily fooled and are susceptible to adversarial attacks \cite{szegedy2013intriguing, nguyen2015deep, Foolllmandvision , and even when the adversarial attacks fail, the saliency interpretations of deep neural networks (DNNs) are rather brittle \cite{carbone2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: When data variations leading to out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts occur neural networks often fail to generalize well \cite{hein2019relu, zhang2024featurecontaminationneuralnetworks, ashukha2020pitfalls .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Moreover, standard neural networks usually lack intuitive interpretation and explainability and so are regarded as black boxes \cite{lipton2018mythos .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To address these challenges, Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) have emerged as a compelling extension of conventional neural networks (for a review, see e.g. \cite{jospin2022hands,arbel2023primer ).}
elements.sent-2411-015: While finite (non-Bayesian) deep ensembles of independent neural networks have been shown to improve prediction and uncertainty estimates \cite{LakshminarayananDeepEns , the Bayesian approach creates infinite ensembles of deep neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The advantage of this approach is that it controls the model complexity and builds regularization into the model by marginalizing out the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-017: Indeed, Bayesian neural networks have become the gold standard for uncertainty estimation in the context of data-driven decision-making and in safety-critical applications, where robustness and calibration are crucial \cite{mcallister2017concrete, carbone2020,gruver2023protein, yang2023sneakypromptjailbreakingtexttoimagegenerative, klarner2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: A core problem of Bayesian machine learning lies in performing the inference; in practice, the posterior distribution of the model's parameters given observations is not available in the closed form and direct sampling from the posterior is computationally expensive, meaning one has to employ approximate Bayesian inference.
elements.sent-2411-019: Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) is a gold standard solution since it produces draws, which are asymptotically exact samples from the posterior but for large data sets or complex models with multimodal posteriors it can be prohibitively slow.
elements.sent-2411-020: Variational inference \cite{hinton1993keeping, Jordan1999 (VI) instead utilizes optimization rather than sampling making it a more computationally effective method suitable for high-dimensional problems.}
elements.sent-2411-021: VI approximates the posterior with the closest (most commonly, in terms of the Kullback–Leibler divergence) member of some tractable variational family of distributions taken as close as possible to the true posterior \cite{blei2017variational .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Recently, several variational algorithms and methods have been proposed and proven to achieve desirable consistency and predictive performance \cite{ bai2020efficient, zhang2018advances, castillo2024posterior, yang2020alpha, cherief2020convergence .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we follow the setup of \cite{smithbayesian and introduce a bow tie neural network, where a stochastic relaxation of the rectified linear unit (ReLU) activation function leads to a model amendable to the Polya-Gamma (PG) data augmentation trick \cite{polson2013bayesian and results in conditionally linear and Gaussian stochastic activations.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, on the weights of the network, we place sparsity-inducing priors, which are known for their ability to provide improvement in the predictive performance of Bayesian deep models; not only do sparse models ease the storage and computational burden, they also improve the calibration and may recover the potential sparse structure of the target function \cite{polson2018posterior, bai2020efficient, griffin2021bayesian, LawZankin, szaborayvbforsparse .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, we consider sparsity-inducing global-local normal-generalized inverse Gaussians (N-GIG) priors \cite{polson2010shrink .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Having constructed the bow tie neural network, we propose a (block) structured mean-field family for the approximate variational posterior which is flexible enough and doesn't require assumptions on the distributional form of each component as well as on independence across layers.
elements.sent-2411-027: For the chosen family, coordinate ascent variation inference (CAVI) \cite{bishop2016pattern can be performed, with all variational updates available in the closed form.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Whilst continuous shrinkage priors result in more tractable computations, they do not incur exact zeros on the neural network's weights.
elements.sent-2411-029: We address this issue by implementing a simple post-process node selection algorithm controlled by the empirical Bayesian false discovery rate (FDR).
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we derive the predictive distribution and propose improving the accuracy and uncertainty estimation by considering ensembles of variational approximations obtained by running several parallel variational algorithms with different random starting points.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this way, our approach accounts for the multimodality of the posterior distributions arising in Bayesian deep models.
elements.sent-2411-032: We evaluate our method on a range of classical regression tasks as well as synthetic regression tasks and demonstrate its competitiveness compared to alternative well-known Bayesian algorithms in \cref{sec:experiments .}
elements.section-2411-002: Bayesian Augmented Bow Tie Neural Network with Shrinkage
elements.sent-2411-033: We begin by describing the class of recently proposed bow tie networks \cite{smithbayesian , which are deep generative models that generalize feed-forward rectified linear neural networks with stochastic activations.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Let $\bx_n \in \R^{D_0}$ be the inputs, $\by_n \in \R^{D_{L+1 }$ be the outputs and $\ba_n = \{\ba_{n,l}\}_{l=1 ^{L}$ with $\ba_{n,l \in \R^{D_l}$ be the latent activations at each of the $L$ intermediate layers.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The model assumes: \begin{equation}\by_n \mid \ba_n, \bx_n, \btheta \sim \Norm \left(\by_n | \bz_{n,L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1} \right) \quad  for  n=1, \ldots, N,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\ba_{n,l} |\bz_{n,l}, \btheta \sim \Norm \left( f(\bz_{n,l}), \bSigma_{l} \right), \quad with \quad \bz_{n,l} = \bW_l \ba_{n,l-1} + \bb_l \quad for \quad l=1,\ldots, L+1. \label{eq:bnn_stochastic}\end{equation} Here $f(\bz)$ is a nonlinear activation function applied elementwise and the parameters $\btheta = (\bW_l, \bb_l, \bSigma_l)_{l=1 ^{L+1}$ consist of the weights $\bW_l \in \R^{D_l \times D_{l-1 }$, biases $\bb_l \in \R^{D_l}$ and covariance matrices $\bSigma_l \in \R^{D_l \times D_l}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Note that \cref{eq:bnn_stochastic is a stochastic relaxation of the standard feed-forward NN, which is recovered in the limiting case when $\bSigma_l \rightarrow \bzero $ for $l=1,\ldots, L$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Instead of relying on local gradient-based algorithms, \cite{smithbayesian introduces another relaxation of the model and employs a Polya-Gamma data augmentation trick \cite{polson2013bayesian to render the model conditionally linear with Gaussian activations.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Specifically, consider the ReLU activation function $f(z) = \max(0,z)$.
elements.sent-2411-040: It can alternatively be written as a product of $z$ and a binary function $\gamma$, i.e. $f(z) = \gamma z$ where $\gamma = \1(z>0)$.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this way, $\gamma$ determines whether the node is activated ($\gamma =1$) or not ($\gamma = 0$).
elements.sent-2411-042: In a similar fashion, the additional stochastic relaxation replaces $f(\bz_{n,l )$ with $\bgamma_{n,l \odot \bz_{n,l $: \begin{equation}\ba_{n,l} |\bz_{n,l}, \bgamma_{n,l},\btheta \sim \Norm \left( \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right), \gamma_{n,l,d} \indsim \Bern\left(\sigma\left( z_{n,l,d}/\Tau\right) \right),\end{equation} where $\Tau \geq 0$ is the temperature parameter, $\sigma(x) = \exp(x)/(1+\exp(x)) $ is the logistic function and $\odot$ represents the elementwise product.}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Thus, the nodes are turned off or on stochastically depending on their input.

elements.sent-2411-046: The density resembles a bow tie, hence the name of the model.

elements.sent-2411-047: Prior elicitation in Bayesian neural networks is challenging, as understanding how the high-dimensional weights map to the functions implemented by the network is not trivial.
elements.sent-2411-048: Standard Gaussian priors are often a default choice, also due to their link with $\ell_2$ regularization in maximum a posteriori (MAP) inference; indeed, such priors were used in \cite{smithbayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-049: For an overview and discusson on priors in Bayesian neural networks, see \cite{fortuin2022priors .}
elements.sent-2411-050: We take an alternative approach to the Gaussian priors of \cite{smithbayesian in order to sparsify our model.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Sparsity-inducing priors ease the problem of storage and computational costs, have been shown to provide improvement in the predictive performance of deep models, and can provide a data-driven approach to selecting the width and depth, easing the difficult task of specifying the network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-052: For these reasons, such priors have been considered in a large number of works to produce sparse BNNs.
elements.sent-2411-053: For classical proposals on the two-group discrete mixture priors with a point mass at zero (referred to as spike-and-slab priors) in high-dimensional regression, see \cite{george1993variable,mitchell1988bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-054: More recently, many consider spike-and-slab priors on the neural network weights and provide both theoretical guarantees and demonstrate empirical improvements \cite{blundell2015weight, polson2018posterior, bai2020efficient, sun2022learning, lee2022asymptotic .}
elements.sent-2411-055: As an alternative to spike-and-slab priors, shrinkage priors employ a single distribution to approximate the spike-and-slab shape, yet are more computationally attractive, as they avoid exploring the space of all possible models (corresponding to selecting subsets of inputs/nodes).
elements.sent-2411-056: Moreover, (nearly) optimal theoretical guarantees of spike-and-slab priors can still be obtained with shrinkage priors in the linear regression setting \cite{song2017nearly .}
elements.sent-2411-057: A popular choice of shrinkage prior is the horseshoe \cite{carvalho09a, PiironenHorseshoe , and imposing regularized horseshoe priors on the BNN weights combined with a structured variational approximation provides competitive empirical results \cite{ghosh2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Another example arising in neural networks is a Gaussian scale mixture prior with automatic relevance determination, which when combined with suitable approximate Bayesian inference is equivalent to introducing dropout regularization in NN \cite{nalisnick2019dropout .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Recently, \cite{castillo2024posterior shows that a suitably rescaled heavy-tailed prior on the neural network weights achieves automatic adaptation, simultaneously to both the intrinsic dimension and smoothness of the underlying function, and near-optimal minimax contraction rates of the fractional posterior distribution and its mean-field variational approximation.}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this work, we focus on a class of continuous shrinkage priors, namely, global-local normal scale-mixtures with generalized inverse Gaussian shrinkage priors on the scale parameters, referred to as global-local normal-generalized inverse Gaussian priors \cite{griffin2021bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Global-local scale-mixtures aim to shrink less important weights whilst leaving large ones, which is achieved through a global parameter controlling the overall shrinkage, with the local parameters allowing deviations at the level of individual nodes \cite{polson2010shrink,bhadra2019lasso .}
elements.sent-2411-062: This choice of priors is also motivated by the theoretical guarantees for high-dimensional regression \cite{song2017nearly, griffinbrownInfnormgamma, polson2013bayesian , for a survey on global-local shrinkage methods we refer to \cite{griffin2021bayesian .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The N-GIG priors on the weights have the following hierarchical structure: \begin{equation}\Prob(\bW_l | \bpsi_{l} , \tau_l) = \prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \prod_{d'=1}^{D_{l-1}}\Norm\left(W_{l,d,d'} | 0, \tau_l \psi_{l,d,d'} \right), \label{eq:normalGIG} \Prob(\bpsi_{l} ) =\prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \prod_{d'=1}^{D_{l-1}} \GIG\left( \psi_{l,d,d'} \mid \nu_{\loc, l}, \delta_{\loc, l}, \lambda_{\loc, l} \right), \label{eq:lGIG} \Prob(\tau_{l} ) =\GIG\left( \tau_l \mid \nu_{\glob}, \delta_{\glob}, \lambda_{\glob} \right), \label{eq:glGIG}\end{equation} where $\tau_{l}$ is the global shrinkage parameter for layer $l$ and $\psi_{l,d,d'}$ is the local shrinkage parameter for each weight.
elements.sent-2411-064:
The generalized inverse Gaussian (GIG) prior is given by:  egin{equation}\GIG\left( \psi \mid 
u, \delta, \lambda ight) \propto \psi^{
u-1} \exp \left( - rac{1}{2} (\delta^2/\psi + \lambda^2\psi) ight),\end{equation} with parameters $
u$, $\delta$, and $\lambda$; for a proper prior, $
u>0$ if $\delta= 0$ or $
u<0$ if $\lambda=0$.
elements.sent-2411-065: In \cref{eq:lGIG , we allow the GIG parameters for the local scale parameters $\psi_{l,d,d'}$ to vary across layers to adjust local shrinkage for wider layers.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Furthermore, to encourage more shrinkage for larger depth and width, we scale the global parameters $\tau_{l}$ with respect to $L$ and the local parameters $\psi_{l,d,d'}$ with respect to $D_{l}$ (details of our approach are provided \cref{sec:initschemes ).}
elements.sent-2411-067: When the global shrinkage parameter $\tau_l$ is fixed, examples of the marginal distribution for $w_{l,d,d'}$ include Laplace \cite{parkcasellalasso , Student-t (ST) \cite{TippingSparse , Normal-Gamma (NG) \cite{caronsparse, griffinbrownInfnormgamma , Normal inverse Gaussian (NIG) \cite{caronsparse .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Each example has a different tail behavior, inducing different forms of shrinkage (see \cref{tab:examples for a overview and \cref{fig:priors (a) for a visualization).}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Note that if the prior is polynomial-tailed, then for large signals the amount of shrinkage is mitigated even given small $\tau_l$ \cite{polson2010shrink .}

elements.sent-2411-072: The opposite effects of varying width and depth in deep neural networks are studied in \cite{vladimirova2021 ; while depth accentuates a model’s non-Gaussianity, the width makes models increasingly Gaussian.}

elements.sent-2411-074: Gaussian weights \cite{neal,lee18,matthews and relaxing these assumptions, e.g. through ordering, constraints, heavy tails, or bottlenecks, results in non-Gaussian limits, such as stable processes \cite{peluchetti2020 , deep GPs \cite{agrawal2020wide or more exotic processes \cite{sell2020dimension,chada2022multilevel .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The sparsity promoting priors in \cref{eq:normalGIG,eq:lGIG,eq:glGIG provide a framework for the data to inform on the width and depth of the network.}
elements.sent-2411-076: As in \cite{smithbayesian , we employ Polya-Gamma data augmentation \cite{polson2013bayesian to render the model conditionally linear and Gaussian.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: First, recall the definition of the Polya-Gamma distribution with parameters $b>0$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$, denoted $\PG(b,c)$.
elements.sent-2411-078: The random variable $X \sim \PG(b,c)$ if \begin{equation}X \eqd \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \sum_{k=1}^\infty \frac{g_k}{(k-1/2)^2 + c^2/4\pi^2}, \quad \text {where } g_k \iidsim \Gam(b,1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-079: The key identity that we use is: \begin{equation}\frac{\exp(z)^a}{(1+\exp(z))^b} = 2^{-b} \exp(\kappa z) \int_0^\infty \exp(-\frac{\omega z^2}{2} ) p(\omega) d\omega, \label{eq:pgidentity}\end{equation} where $\kappa = a-b/2$ and $p(\omega) = PG(\omega| b,0)$.
elements.sent-2411-080: The integral is a Gaussian kernel, thus if $z=\bw^T\bx$, conditioned on the latent variable $\omega$, $\bw$ has a Gaussian distribution and conditioned on $\bw$, $\omega$ has a PG distribution.
elements.sent-2411-081: While to sample from the PG distribution, one can use the alternating series method of \cite{devroye2006nonuniform , all finite moments of the PG random variables are available in closed form and that becomes useful for expectation-maximization or variational Bayes algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, for $c>0$ \begin{equation}\E[\omega] = \frac{b}{2c} \frac{\exp(c)-1}{1+\exp(c)}. \label{eq:pgmean}\end{equation} Moreover, the PG distribution is closed under convolution with the same scale parameter; if $\omega_1 \sim \PG(b_1,c)$ and $\omega_2 \sim \PG(b_2,c)$, then $\omega_1 + \omega_2 \sim \PG(b_1+b_2,c)$.
elements.sent-2411-083: The model described in \cref{sec:bowtie augmented with stochastic activations $\ba =(\ba_{n,l )$ and binary activations $\bgamma = (\bgamma_{n,l )$ is: \begin{equation}p(\by, \ba, \bgamma |\btheta) = \prod_{n=1}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1}\right) \prod_{n=1}^N \prod_{l=1}^L \Norm \left(\ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right) \times \prod_{d=1}^{D_l}\frac{\exp(z_{n,l,d}/T )^{\gamma_{n,l,d}}}{1+\exp(z_{n,l,d}/T )}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Then using the \cref{eq:pgidentity , the last term can be written as: \begin{equation}\frac{\exp(z_{n,l,d}/T )^{\gamma_{n,l,d}}}{1+\exp(z_{n,l,d}/T)} = 2^{-1} \exp \left (\frac{\kappa_{n,l,d} z_{n,l,d}}{T}\right ) \int_0^\infty \exp \left (-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2} \right ) p(\omega_{n,l,d}) d\omega_{n,l,d},\end{equation} where $\omega_{n,l,d \sim \PG(1,0)$ and $\kappa_{n,l,d = \gamma_{n,l,d - 1/2$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, introducing the additional augmented variables $\bomega = (\omega_{n,l,d )$, we arrive at the augmented model: \begin{equation}p(\by, \ba, \bgamma,\bomega |\btheta) \propto \prod_{n=1}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1}\right) \prod_{n=1}^N \prod_{l=1}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right) \times\prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \exp \left (\frac{\kappa_{n,l,d} z_{n,l,d}}{T}\right ) \exp \left (-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2} \right ) p(\omega_{n,l,d}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-086: To ease computations, the covariance matrices are assumed to be diagonal $\bSigma_l = \diag(\eta_{l,1 ^2,\ldots \eta_{l,D_l ^2)$, and the variances are denoted by $\bmeta_l=(\eta_{l,1 ^2,\ldots \eta_{l,D_l ^2)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Additionally, we assume conjugate priors for the variances $\eta_{l,d ^2 \iidsim \IG(\alpha^h_0,\beta^h_0)$ for $l=1, \ldots, L$ and $\eta_{L+1,d ^2 \iidsim \IG(\alpha_0,\beta_0)$ and for the biases $b_{l,d \iidsim \Norm(0, s^2_0)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Here, we consider different prior parameters $\alpha_0^h, \beta_0^h$ for the variance terms associated to the hidden layers in comparison to the prior parameters $\alpha_0, \beta_0$ for the final layer.
elements.sent-2411-089: In particular, $\alpha_0, \beta_0$ are chosen to reflect uncertainty in noise, while $\alpha_0^h, \beta_0^h$ are chosen so that prior concentrates on small values and realizations of the stochastic activation function are more similar to the ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-090: A graphical model of the bow tie BNN with stochastic relaxation and shrinkage priors is displayed in \cref{fig:DAG , and the posterior distribution over both the model parameters and latent variables is: \begin{equation}p(\ba, \bgamma,\bomega,\bW, \bb, \bmeta,\bpsi,\btau) \propto\prod_{n=1}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1}\right) \prod_{n=1}^N \prod_{l=1}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right) \times \prod_{n=1}^N \prod_{l=1}^L\prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \exp \left (\frac{\kappa_{n,l,d} z_{n,l,d}}{T}\right ) \exp \left (-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2} \right ) p(\omega_{n,l,d}) \times \prod_{n=1}^N \prod_{l=1}^L \prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \Bern \left(\gamma_{n,l,d} \mid \sigma \left (\frac{z_{n,l,d}}{T} \right ) \right) \times \prod_{d=1}^{D_{L+1}}\IG(\eta^2_{L+1,d} \mid \alpha_0, \beta_0) \times \prod_{l=1}^L \prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \IG( \eta^2_{l,d} \mid \alpha_0^h, \beta_0^h) \times \prod_{l=1}^L \left( \prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \left( \Norm(b_{l,d} \mid 0, s_0^2) \times \prod_{d'=1}^{D_{l-1}}\Norm\left(W_{l,d,d'} | 0, \tau_l \psi_{l,d,d'} \right)\right)\right) \times \prod_{l=1}^L \left( \GIG\left( \tau_l \mid \nu_{\glob}, \delta_{\glob}, \lambda_{\glob} \right) \prod_{d=1}^{D_l} \prod_{d'=1}^{D_{l-1}} \GIG\left( \psi_{l,d,d'} \mid \nu_{\loc,l}, \delta_{\loc,l}, \lambda_{\loc,l} \right) \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-091: While Markov chain Monte Carlo is considered the gold-standard tool for approximating posterior distributions in Bayesian modelling due its asymptotic guarantees, MCMC algorithms can be prohibitively slow when the model dimension and sample size are large.
elements.sent-2411-092: An alternative fast approximate Bayesian inference method known as variational inference has gained popularity in the literature \cite{Ormerod2010, zhang2018advances , due to both the explosion in the amount of data collected and use of highly parametrized models for increased flexibility.}
elements.sent-2411-093: VI has been shown to yield reasonably accurate approximations in several problems as well as desirable frequentist properties.
elements.sent-2411-094: Namely, consistency and asymptotic normality of VI are studied in \cite{wang2019frequentist , theoretical guarantees for optimal contraction rates of variational posteriors under certain assumptions appear in several recent works \cite{zhang2020convergence,alquier2020concentration, bhattacharya2023convergence, yang2020alpha and contraction rates as well as model selection consistency in mixtures are considered in \cite{cherief2020convergence .}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Several works focus on properties of VI approximation in sparse models: oracle contraction rates of a mean-field VI in the case of high-dimensional regression are established in \cite{szaborayvbforsparse and in the case of the neural networks with spike-and-slab priors, contraction rates of variational posteriors are studied in \cite{bai2020efficient .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: More recently, near-optimal contraction rates of the mean-field VI approximations are obtained in the context of neural networks with heavy-tailed priors on the weights \cite{castillo2024posterior .}
elements.sent-2411-097: We refer readers interested in the caveats of VI to \cite{gelmanyesbutdidit .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Consider fitting a model parameterized by $\btheta$ to the observed data $\mathcal{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-099: In variational inference, the true posterior $p(\btheta \mid \mathcal{D )$ is approximated by a density $q(\btheta)$ taken from a family of distributions $\mathcal{F}$ that minimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the approximate and true posterior, or equivalently maximizes the evidence lower bound (ELBO) \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ELBO} \text{ELBO} = \E_{q(\btheta) } \left [ \log \left( \frac{p(\btheta, \mathcal{D}) }{q(\btheta)} \right)\right ].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-100: A common choice for $\mathcal{F}$ is the mean-field family on a partition $\{ \btheta_1, \ldots, \btheta_J \}$ of $\btheta$, assuming that the variational posterior factorizes over (blocks) of latent variables: $ q(\btheta) = \prod_{j=1 ^J q_j(\btheta_j), $ where $J \le \text{dim (\btheta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Without any further parametric assumptions, it has been shown \cite{hinton1993keeping,mackay1995probable,Jordan1999 that the optimal choice for each product component $q_j$ is \begin{equation}\label{eqn::CAVIupdate} q_j (\btheta_j) \propto \exp \left [ \E_{- \btheta_j} \log \left( p(\btheta, \mathcal{D}) \right) \right ],\end{equation} where the above expectation is taken with respect to $\prod_{j^\prime \neq j q_{j^\prime (\btheta_{j^\prime )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The process of sweeping through the components of the partition and updating one at a time via \cref{eqn::CAVIupdate is known as coordinate ascent variational inference (CAVI).}
elements.sent-2411-103: Limitations of the mean-field assumption can be found in \cite{wand2011mean, Neville_2014, coker2022wide .}
elements.sent-2411-104: We specify the mean-field family for the approximate variational posterior: \begin{equation}q(\ba, \bgamma,\bomega,\bW, \bb, \bmeta,\bpsi,\btau) = q(\ba) q(\bgamma) q(\bomega)q(\bW,\bb)q(\bmeta)q(\bpsi)q(\btau).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-105: Note that the assumption on the family above could be referred to as the strucutred mean-field assumtion.
elements.sent-2411-106: Importantly, unlike existing variational algorithms for BNNs, we do not make any assumptions on independence of parameters between layers.
elements.sent-2411-107: The calculation of each component of the variational posterior is given in the \cref{appendix:vb , where using \cref{eqn::CAVIupdate we obtain the following update steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: And for the final layer, the updated variational parameters for $d=1, \ldots, D_{L+1}$ are given by \begin{equation}\alpha_{L+1,d} = \alpha_0 + \frac{N}{2}, \beta_{L+1,d} = \beta_0 + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{n}^N \left( y_{n,d} - \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d} \right ] \E [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n,L}] \right)^2 +\frac{1}{2} \sum_n^N \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d}^T \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n,L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n,L}^T\right] \right) - \Tr \left (\E [ \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d}]^T \E[\widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d} ] \E [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n,L}] \E[\widetilde{\ba}_{n,L}^T]\right ).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-126: The choice of the network architecture has significant practical implications on the generalization of the model, and so sparsity-promoting priors for the network weights have emerged as promising approach to allow for a data-driven choice of architecture.
elements.sent-2411-127: Instead of the classical two-group discrete mixture priors, in this article, we focus on a class of continuous shrinkage priors.
elements.sent-2411-128: While this results in more tractable computations, it also implies non-zero posterior means and draws and doesn't lead to automatic network architecture selection.
elements.sent-2411-129: Several post-processing methods have been proposed to yield a sparse solution (see e.g.\cite{Piironen2020Varsel, LI_Varsel, griffin2024expressing ).}
elements.sent-2411-130: The method known as decoupling shrinkage and selection (DSS) \cite{hahn2014decoupling obtains sparse estimates of the weights by minimizing the sum of the predictive loss function with a parsimony-inducing penalty.}
elements.sent-2411-131: An alternative approach is the the penalized credible regions (PenCR) method \cite{Zhang_VarSelect , which identifies the "sparsest" solution in posterior credible regions corresponding to different levels; it is shown to perform well in the case of global-local shrinkage priors and under certain assumptions, PenCR produces the same results as DSS.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Similarly, we propose to make use of credible intervals to select nodes.
elements.sent-2411-133: Following \cite{LiLin2010 , we implement an automatic credible interval criterion which selects a node as long as its credible interval doesn't cover zero.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Specifically, recall that the variational posterior of the weights is $W_{l,d,d' \sim \Norm(m^W_{l,d,d' , B^W_{l,d,d' )$ for $l=1,\ldots,L, d= 1, \dots, D_{l , d' = 1, \ldots, D_{l-1}$, where $\mathbf{m ^W_{l,d}$ and $\mathbf{B ^W_{l,d}$ denote the subsets of the mean $\mathbf{m _{l,d}$ and covariance matrix $\mathbf{B _{l,d}$ corresponding to the weights.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Then, we obtain sparse weights $\widehat{W _{l,d,d'}$ with sparse variational distribution $\widehat{q (b_{l,d , \widehat{\bW _{l,d )$ for some $ l \in \mathcal{L \subseteq \{1, \ldots, L+1\}, \; d \in \mathcal{D _l \subseteq \{ 1, \ldots, D_l\}, d' \in \mathcal{D _{l-1 \subseteq \{ 1, \ldots, D_{l-1}\}$, defined by setting: \begin{equation}\widehat{W}_{l,d,d'} \sim  \Norm\left (m^W_{l,d,d'}, \left(B^W_{l,d}\right)_{d'd'}\right )  if  \max \left ( Q(W_{l,d,d'} >0) , Q(W_{l,d,d'}<0)\right ) \geq \kappa, \delta_0  otherwise,  \end{equation} where $Q(W_{l,d,d' <0) = 1- Q(W_{l,d,d' >0) =\Phi(- m^W_{l,d,d' /\sqrt{(B^W_{l,d )_{d'd' )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: The threshold $\kappa$ is chosen to control the Bayesian false discovery rate, which is calculated as \begin{equation}\widehat{FDR}(\kappa) = \frac{\sum_{l,d,d'} (1-Q_{l,d.d'})\1(Q_{l,d.d'}>\kappa) }{\sum_{l,d,d'} \1(Q_{l,d.d'}>\kappa)},\end{equation} with $\mathcal{Q _{l,d.d' = \max \left ( Q(W_{l,d,d' >0) , Q(W_{l,d,d' <0)\right )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Specifically, for a specified error rate $\alpha$, $\kappa$ is set to satisfy $ \widehat{\text{FDR (\kappa)< \alpha$.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Node selection algorithm} \label{alg:vas} \begin{algorithmic} \REQUIRE $\mathcal{I} = \{ \mathcal{Q}_{l,d.d'} \mid l = 1 \ldots L, \; d = 1 \ldots D_{l+1}, \; d' = 1 \ldots D_{l} \}$. \STATE{$\hat{\kappa} = \max (\mathcal{I})$} \STATE{$\mathcal{I} = \mathcal{I}\setminus \hat{\kappa}$} \IF{$\widehat{\text{FDR}}(\max (\mathcal{I}))< \alpha$} \STATE{$\hat{\kappa} = \max (\mathcal{I})$} \STATE{$\mathcal{I} = \mathcal{I}\setminus \hat{\kappa}$} \ELSE \STATE{break} \ENDIF \FOR{ $l = 1 \ldots L, \; d = 1 \ldots D_{l+1}, \; d' = 1 \ldots D_{l}$} \IF{$\mathcal{Q}_{l,d,d'} \geq \hat{\kappa}$} \STATE{$\widehat{W}_{l,d,d'} \sim \Norm\left (m^W_{l,d,d'}, \left(B^W_{l,d}\right)_{d'd'}\right )$} \ELSE \STATE{$\widehat{W}_{l,d,d'} = 0$ a.s.} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \FOR{ $l = L+1,\ldots 2,d = 1,\ldots D_l,$} \IF{$\widehat{W}_{l,d}= 0$ a.s.} \STATE{$\widehat{W}_{l-1,d',d} = 0 \text{ a.s.} \;\forall \; d' = 1, \ldots, D_{l-1}$ } \ELSE \IF{$\widehat{W}_{l-1,d',d} = 0 \text{ a.s.} \; \forall \; d' = 1, \ldots, D_{l-1}$} \STATE{$\widehat{W}_{l,d} = 0$ a.s. } \ENDIF \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENSURE $\widehat{q}(b_{l,d}, \widehat{\bW}_{l,d}), \; l \in \mathcal{L}, \; d \in \mathcal{D}_l, \; d' \in \mathcal{D}_{l-1}.$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-146: Finally, the Polya-Gamma augmented variables are independent across layers $l=1,\ldots, L$ and width $d=1, \ldots, D_l$, with a Polya-Gamma variational predictive distribution: \begin{equation}q(\bomega_*) = \prod_{l}^{L} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \PG( \omega_{*,l,d} \mid 1, A_{*,l,d}), \label{eq:vbomegapred}\end{equation} with updated variational parameters: \begin{equation}A_{*,l,d} = \frac{1}{T} \sqrt{\left ( \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{*,l-1} \widetilde{\ba}_{*,l-1}^T\right ] \right ) \right )}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-152: Specifically, the expectation of $\by_*$ under the variational predictive distribution is: \begin{equation}\E[\by_* \mid \bx_*, D] \approx \E_{q_{L+1}}[\bW_{L+1}]\E_{q_{*,L}} [\ba_{*,L}] + \E_{q_{L+1}}[\bb_{L+1}], \label{eq:pred_exp}\end{equation} where recursively \begin{equation}\E_{q_{*,L}} [\ba_{*,L}] = \E_{q_{*,L-1}} \left [ \E_{q(\ba_{*,L} | \ba_{*,L-1})}[\ba_{*,L}] \right ] = t_{*,L}+ M_{*,L} E_{q_{*,L-1}}[\ba_{*,L-1}].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-153: Similarly, the variational variance of $\by_*$ is \begin{equation}\Var \left[y_{*, d} \mid \bx_*, D\right] \approx \Var_{q_{L+1}} \left[\bW_{L+1,d}\ba_{*,L} + \bb_{L+1,d}\right] + \E_{q_{L+1}} \left[\bmeta_{L+1,d}^{2}\right],\end{equation} where the first term represents the signal variance and is computed as{ { \begin{equation}\Var_{q_{L+1}} \left[\bW_{L+1,d} \ba_{*,L} + \bb_{L+1,d} \right] =\E_{q_{L+1}} \left[(\bW_{L+1,d}\ba_{*,L} + \bb_{L+1,d})^2\right] - \left(\E_{q_{L+1}}\left[\bW_{L+1,d} \right] \E_{q_{L}}\left[\ba_{*,L} \right] + \E_{q_{L+1}}\left[\bb_{L+1,d}\right]\right)^{2} = \Tr \left( \E_{q_{L+1}} \left[\bW_{L+1,d}^T\bW_{L+1,d}\right]\E_{q_{L}} \left[\ba_{*,L} \ba_{*,L}^T\right] - \E_{q_{L+1}} \left[\bW_{L+1,d}\right]^T\E_{q_{L+1}} \left[\bW_{L+1,d}\right]\E_{q_{L}} \left[\ba_{*,L}\right] \E_{q_{L}} \left[\ba_{*,L}\right]^T\right) +\Var_{q_{L+1}}(\bb_{L+1,d}) + 2Cov_{q_{L+1}}(\bW_{L+1,d}, \bb_{L+1,d}) \E_{q_{L}}\left[\ba_{*,L} \right],\end{equation} which requires the recursive computation: \begin{equation}\E_{q_{L}} \left[\ba_{*,L} \ba_{*,L}^T\right] = \bS_{*, L} + \bt_{*, L} \bt_{*, L}^T + 2\bM_{*,L} \E_{q_{L-1}} \left[\ba_{*,L-1} \right ] \bt_{*,L}^T + \bM_{*,L} \E_{q_{L-1}} \left[\ba_{*,L-1} \ba_{*,L-1}^T \right ] \bM_{*,L}^T.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-156: While the variational algorithm described in \cref{sec:vb increases the ELBO at each epoch, the ELBO is a non-convex function of the variational parameters and only convergence to a local optimum is guaranteed.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Due to identifiability issues, the posterior distribution of a Bayesian neural network is highly multimodal, and exploring this posterior is notoriously challenging \cite{papamarkou2022challenges .}
elements.sent-2411-158: A single variational approximation tends to concentrate around one mode and can understate posterior uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-159: Several approaches have been proposed to overcome such issues.
elements.sent-2411-160: Recently, \cite{ohn2024adaptive introduce adaptive variational inference which achieves optimal posterior contraction rate and model selection consistency by considering several variational approximations obtained in different models.}
elements.sent-2411-161: In a similar but simpler fashion to the above proposal, we consider an ensemble of variational approximations, obtained by running in parallel the variational algorithm multiple times with different random starting points.
elements.sent-2411-162: In this case, letting $k=1,\ldots, K$ index the different variational approximations, we compute the weight $w_k$ associated with each approximation in accordance with the optimization objective, the ELBO: \begin{equation}w_k \propto \exp \left (ELBO_k\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-163: We can interpret this as a Bayesian model averaging across the $K$ different models/approximations.
elements.sent-2411-164: While in an ideal setting, the weights would be proportional to the marginal likelihood for each model, the use of the ELBO is motivated as it provides a lower bound to the marginal likelihood and can be computed in closed form.
elements.sent-2411-165: Next, we compute predictions by taking a weighted average of the predictive distributions of each model (given in \cref{eq:pred_exp ), that is \begin{equation}\E[\by_* \mid \bx_*, D] \approx \sum_{k=1}^K w_k \E_{q_k}[\by_* \mid \bx_*, D],\end{equation} where each expectation is taken with respect to $q_k$ (the $k$th variational approximation).}

D]\right)^2.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-169: We evaluate the variational bow tie neural network (VBNN) on several datasets.
elements.sent-2411-170: First, we consider a simple nonlinear synthetic example to compare with a ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-171: We then validate VBNN on the \cmsspy{diabetes dataset, first considered in \cite{efron2004least to demonstrate the least angle regression (LARS) algorithm for variable selection, and subsequently, used in different proposals for sparsity-promoting priors and algorithms (e.g. \cite{parkcasellalasso, LiLin2010 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Lastly, we consider a range of popular regression datasets from the UCI Machine Learning Repository \cite{UCIMLrep .}
elements.sent-2411-173: The importance of suitable initialization choice in NNs is well known \cite{wenzel2020goodbayesposteriordeep, Daniely2016init, he2015delvingdeeprectifierssurpassing , and we design two possible random initialization schemes of the VBNN, which are described in \cref{sec:initschemes and used in all experiments.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Convergence of the ELBO is monitored during the training and prediction stages, where if three consecutive measurements of ELBO for training differ by less than the specified threshold, the phase is stopped and the model moves to the prediction stage, where we proceed similarly.
elements.sent-2411-175: In most experiments, the thresholds during the training and prediction stages are set, respectively, to $1e-5$ and $1e-4$.
elements.sent-2411-176: We compare the performance of VBNN to two popular variational frameworks under the mean-field assumption: the stochastic variational inference approximation (SVI) implemented in \cmsspy{Numpyro \cite{numpyrophan2019composable , and Bayes by Backprop (BBB) \cite{blundell2015weight implemented with \cmsspy{Pytorch .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: For all the datasets, we evaluate the performance over 10 random splits, where we use $90\%$ of the data for the training and $10\%$ for testing the model.
elements.sent-2411-178: We normalize the input but do not re-scale the output, we record the root mean squared error (RMSE), the predictive negative log-likelihood of the test data (NLL) and the empirical coverage, see \cref{sec:experimentsap for additional implementation details and the definition of each metric.}
elements.sent-2411-179: We construct a synthetic dataset generated by first uniformly sampling a two-dimensional input vector $\bx_n = (x_{n,1 , x_{n,2 )$, with $x_{n,d \sim \Unif([-2,2])$, and assume only the first feature influences the output: $y_n = f(x_{n,1 ) + \epsilon_n = 0.1 x_{n,1 ^2 + 10\sin(x_{n,1 ) + \epsilon_n$, where $\epsilon_n \sim \Norm(0, 0.5)$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-200: In this paper, we presented a variational bow tie neural network (VBNN) that is amendable to Polya-gamma data augmentation so that the variational inference can be performed via the CAVI algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-201: While the idea of the stochastic relaxation described in \cref{sec:bowtie was introduced in \cite{smithbayesian , the novelty of our model is in the employment of the variational inference techniques as well as sparsity-inducing priors.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Namely, we implement continuous global-local shrinkage priors and propose a post-process technique for node selection.
elements.sent-2411-203: Additionally, we consider an improvement of the classical CAVI algorithm by adding EM steps for critical hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-204: In this way, we enrich the class of models which are handled within the structured mean-field paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-205: We provide all the necessary (computations), techniques, and illustrative experiments demonstrating the utility of the model.
elements.sent-2411-206: At each iteration, CAVI has to cycle through the entire data set, which can be computationally expensive and inefficient for large sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-207: An alternative to coordinate ascent is gradient-based optimization and a future direction of this research will extend the algorithm by employing Stochastic variational inference \cite{hoffman2013stochastic and subsampling.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Moreover, an extension to other output types, such as classification tasks, will be developed through additional Polya-gamma augmentation techniques \cite{durante2019 .. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}}
elements.sent-2411-211: Under the assumption of a diagonal covariance matrix, with parameters $\bmeta_l=(\eta_{l,1 ^2,\ldots \eta_{l,D_l ^2)$, the variational posterior is: \begin{equation}q(\bmeta) \propto \exp \left ( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1}\right) +\log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right) \right ) \right ) \times \prod_{l}^{L} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \IG(\eta_{l,d}^2 \mid \alpha^h_0, \beta^h_0) \prod_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \IG(\eta_{l,d}^2 \mid \alpha_0, \beta_0) \propto \exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2}\E \left [\sum_{n}^N \sum_{d}^{D_{L+1}}(\eta_{L+1, d})^{-2} \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \prod_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \left ( (\eta_{L+1,d}^2)^{-\alpha_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}}\exp \left ( -\frac{\beta_0 }{ \eta^2_{L+1,d}} \right )\right ) \times \prod_{l}^{L} \exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2}\E \left [\sum_{n}^N \sum_{d}^{D_l}(\eta_{l, d})^{-2} \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \odot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \times \prod_{l}^{L} \prod_{d}^{D_{l}} \left ( (\eta_{l,d}^2)^{-\alpha^h_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}}\exp \left ( -\frac{\beta^h_0 }{ \eta^2_{l,d}} \right )\right ) \propto \prod_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \left ( (\eta_{L+1,d}^2)^{-\alpha_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}} \right ) \exp \left ( - \frac{1}{\eta^2_{L+1,d}} \left ( \beta_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N\E \left [ \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right ) \right) \times \prod_{l}^{L}\prod_{d}^{D_l} (\eta_{l,d}^2)^{-\alpha^h_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}} \exp \left ( - \frac{1}{\eta^{2}_{l,d}} \left (\beta^h_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N \E \left [ \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \odot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \right ).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-046: \begin{equation}q(\bmeta) \propto \exp \left ( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1}\right) +\log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right) \right ) \right ) \times \prod_{l}^{L} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \IG(\eta_{l,d}^2 \mid \alpha^h_0, \beta^h_0) \prod_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \IG(\eta_{l,d}^2 \mid \alpha_0, \beta_0) \propto \exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2}\E \left [\sum_{n}^N \sum_{d}^{D_{L+1}}(\eta_{L+1, d})^{-2} \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \prod_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \left ( (\eta_{L+1,d}^2)^{-\alpha_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}}\exp \left ( -\frac{\beta_0 }{ \eta^2_{L+1,d}} \right )\right ) \times \prod_{l}^{L} \exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2}\E \left [\sum_{n}^N \sum_{d}^{D_l}(\eta_{l, d})^{-2} \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \odot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \times \prod_{l}^{L} \prod_{d}^{D_{l}} \left ( (\eta_{l,d}^2)^{-\alpha^h_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}}\exp \left ( -\frac{\beta^h_0 }{ \eta^2_{l,d}} \right )\right ) \propto \prod_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \left ( (\eta_{L+1,d}^2)^{-\alpha_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}} \right ) \exp \left ( - \frac{1}{\eta^2_{L+1,d}} \left ( \beta_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N\E \left [ \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right ) \right) \times \prod_{l}^{L}\prod_{d}^{D_l} (\eta_{l,d}^2)^{-\alpha^h_0 - 1 -\frac{N}{2}} \exp \left ( - \frac{1}{\eta^{2}_{l,d}} \left (\beta^h_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N \E \left [ \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \odot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \right ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-212: Thus, $ q(\bmeta) \propto \prod_l^{L+1 \prod_d^{D_l \IG(\alpha_{l,d , \beta_{l,d )$, where \begin{equation}\alpha_{l,d} = \alpha^h_0+ \frac{N}{2}, \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_l, \quad l=1, \ldots, L, \alpha_{L+1,d} = \alpha_0 + \frac{N}{2}, \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_{L+1}, \beta_{l,d} = \beta^h_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N \E \left [ \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \odot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right], \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_l, \quad l=1, \ldots, L, \beta_{L+1 ,d} = \beta_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N\E \left [ \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right], \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_{L+1}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-047: \begin{equation}\alpha_{l,d} = \alpha^h_0+ \frac{N}{2}, \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_l, \quad l=1, \ldots, L, \alpha_{L+1,d} = \alpha_0 + \frac{N}{2}, \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_{L+1}, \beta_{l,d} = \beta^h_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N \E \left [ \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \odot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right], \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_l, \quad l=1, \ldots, L, \beta_{L+1 ,d} = \beta_0 + \frac {1}{2}\sum_{n}^N\E \left [ \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right], \quad d = 1, \ldots, D_{L+1}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-213: For the parameters $ \beta_{l,d}$, we must compute the sum of squares terms.
elements.sent-2411-214: For the last layer $l=L+1$, this term, for each data point $n$, is given by: \begin{equation}\E \left [ \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right ] =\sum_{n}^N \left( y_{n,d} - \E \left [\bW_{L+1, d}\right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L} \right ] - \E \left [b_{L+1, d} \right ] \right )^2 + \sum_{n}^N \E \left [b_{L+1, d}^2 \right ] - \E \left [b_{L+1, d} \right ]^2 + 2 \E \left [ b_{L+1, d} \bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L}\right] - 2 \E \left [ b_{L+1, d} \right] \E \left [\bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L}\right] + 2\sum_{n}^N \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{L+1,d}^T \bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L} \ba_{n,L}^T\right] \right ) - \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{L+1,d}^T \right ] \E \left [ \bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L}^T\right] \right ).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}\E \left [ \left( y_{n,d} - z_{n,L+1, d} \right )^{2} \right ] =\sum_{n}^N \left( y_{n,d} - \E \left [\bW_{L+1, d}\right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L} \right ] - \E \left [b_{L+1, d} \right ] \right )^2 + \sum_{n}^N \E \left [b_{L+1, d}^2 \right ] - \E \left [b_{L+1, d} \right ]^2 + 2 \E \left [ b_{L+1, d} \bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L}\right] - 2 \E \left [ b_{L+1, d} \right] \E \left [\bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L}\right] + 2\sum_{n}^N \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{L+1,d}^T \bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L} \ba_{n,L}^T\right] \right ) - \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{L+1,d}^T \right ] \E \left [ \bW_{L+1,d} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L} \right ] \E \left [ \ba_{n,L}^T\right] \right ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-215: Instead, for an intermediate layer $l=1,\ldots, L$, the sum of squares term, for each data point $n$, is given by: \begin{equation}\E \left [ \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d}\cdot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right ] = \sum_{n}^N \left( \E \left [a_{n,l,d} \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [ b_{l,d} \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [\bW_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \right ]\right )^2 +\sum_{n}^N\E \left [a^2_{n,l,d} \right ] - \E \left [a_{n,l,d} \right ]^2 + \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [ b_{l,d}^2 \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]^2 \E \left [ b_{l,d} \right ]^2 + \sum_{n}^N\E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{l,d}^T \bW_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \ba_{n,l-1}^T \right ] \right ) - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]^2 \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{l,d}^T \right ] \E \left [\bW_{l,d} \right ] \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \ba_{n,l-1}^T \right ] \right ) + 2 \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]\E \left [ b_{l,d} \bW_{l,d} \right ] \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]^2\E \left [ b_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \bW_{l,d} \right ] \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \right ].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}\E \left [ \left( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d}\cdot z_{n,l, d} \right )^{2} \right ] = \sum_{n}^N \left( \E \left [a_{n,l,d} \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [ b_{l,d} \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [\bW_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \right ]\right )^2 +\sum_{n}^N\E \left [a^2_{n,l,d} \right ] - \E \left [a_{n,l,d} \right ]^2 + \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [ b_{l,d}^2 \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]^2 \E \left [ b_{l,d} \right ]^2 + \sum_{n}^N\E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{l,d}^T \bW_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \ba_{n,l-1}^T \right ] \right ) - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]^2 \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \bW_{l,d}^T \right ] \E \left [\bW_{l,d} \right ] \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \ba_{n,l-1}^T \right ] \right ) + 2 \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]\E \left [ b_{l,d} \bW_{l,d} \right ] \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \right ] - \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ]^2\E \left [ b_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \bW_{l,d} \right ] \left [\ba_{n,l-1} \right ].\end{equation}

\frac{1}{2}) \left (\bW_{l,d}\ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d}\right )}{T } \right ] \right ) \exp \left (\E \left [-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}\left ( \bW_{l,d}\ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d}\right )^2}{2T^2}\right ] \right ) \times \prod_{l}^{L+1} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \prod_{d'}^{D_{l-1}} \exp \left( -\frac{W_{l,d,d'}^2}{2} \E \left [\frac{1}{\tau_l} \right ] \E \left [ \frac{1}{\psi_{l,d,d'}} \right ] \right ) \prod_{l}^{L+1} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp \left( -\frac{b_{l,d}^2}{2s_0^2}\right ).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-050: \begin{equation}q(\bb, \bW)\propto \exp \left( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \Norm \left ( y_n \mid \bW_{L+1} \ba_{n, L} + \bb_{L+1}, \bSigma_{L+1} \right) \prod_{n}^{N} \prod_{l}^{L} \Norm \left ( \ba_{n, l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot (\bW_{l} \ba_{n,l-1} + \bb_{l}), \bSigma_l\right ) \right] \right) \times \exp \left( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp\left(\frac{(\gamma_{n,l,d} - \frac{1}{2}) z_{n,l,d}}{T }\right) \exp\left(-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2}\right) \right ]\right ) \times \exp \left( \E \left [ \log \prod_{l}^{L+1} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \prod_{d'}^{D_{l-1}}\Norm\left(W_{l,d,d'} | 0, \tau_l \psi_{l,d,d'} \right) \right ]\right ) \prod_{l}^{L+1} \prod_{d}^{D_l}\Norm (b_{l,d} \mid 0, s^2_0) \propto \prod_{n}^N \exp \left ( \E \left [ \log \frac{1}{\sqrt{ |\bSigma_{L+1}| }} \right] \right) \exp \left( \E \left [ -\frac {1}{2} \left ( \by_n - \bW_{L+1} \ba_{n, L} - \bb_{L+1} \right )^{T} \bSigma_{L+1}^{-1} \left ( \by_n - \bW_{L+1} \ba_{n, L} - \bb_{L+1} \right ) \right] \right ) \times \prod_{l}^L\prod_{n}^N \exp \left ( \E \left [\log \frac{1}{\sqrt{|\bSigma_{l}| }} \right ] \right ) \exp \left ( \E \left [\left(-\frac {1}{2} \left ( \ba_{n,l} - \bgamma_{n,l}\bW_{l} \ba_{n,l-1} - \bgamma_{n,l} \bb_{l} \right )^{T} \bSigma_{l}^{-1} \left ( \ba_{n,l} - \bgamma_{n,l}\bW_{l} \ba_{n,l-1} - \bgamma_{n,l} \bb_{l} \right )\right) \right ] \right ) \times \prod_{l}^L\prod_{n}^N \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp \left (\E \left [ \frac{(\gamma_{n,l,d} - \frac{1}{2}) \left (\bW_{l,d}\ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d}\right )}{T } \right ] \right ) \exp \left (\E \left [-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}\left ( \bW_{l,d}\ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d}\right )^2}{2T^2}\right ] \right ) \times \prod_{l}^{L+1} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \prod_{d'}^{D_{l-1}} \exp \left( -\frac{W_{l,d,d'}^2}{2} \E \left [\frac{1}{\tau_l} \right ] \E \left [ \frac{1}{\psi_{l,d,d'}} \right ] \right ) \prod_{l}^{L+1} \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp \left( -\frac{b_{l,d}^2}{2s_0^2}\right ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-217: Therefore, using also the fact that $\bSigma_l$ is diagonal, we have that the variational posterior factorizes as $q(\bb, \bW)= \prod_{l ^{L+1 \prod_{d=1 ^{D_l q(b_{l,d , \bW_{l,d )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: We consider the terms $q(b_{l,d , \bW_{l,d )$ for the intermediate layers $l=1,\dots, L$ and $q(b_{L+1,d , \bW_{L+1,d )$ for the last layer separately.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Starting with the last layer $L+1$, we first introduce the matrix \begin{equation}\bD_{L+1,d}^{-1}= \diag \left( s_0^{-2}, \E \left [\tau_{L+1}^{-1}\right ] \E \left [ \psi_{L+1,d,1}^{-1} \right ], \ldots, \E \left [\tau_{L+1}^{-1} \right ] \E \left [\psi_{L+1,d,D_{L}}^{-1} \right ]\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-220: Then, for the variational posterior of the weights and biases for the $d$th dimension of the final layer, we only need to consider the relevant terms: { {\begin{equation}q(b_{L+1,d}, \bW_{L+1,d}) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d} \bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d}^T -\frac {1}{2}\E \left [(\eta_{L+1,d})^{-2} \right ] \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\left ( y_{n,d} - \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L } \right )^{2} \right] \right ) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T -\frac {\E \left [\eta_{L+1,d}^{-2} \right ] }{2}\left ( \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L}^T \right] \right ) \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T - 2 \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N y_n \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \right] \right ) \right )\right ) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \left (\widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left (\bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} +\E \left [\eta_{L+1,d}^{-2} \right ] \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L}^T \right] \right ) \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T - 2 \E \left [\eta_{L+1,d}^{-2} \right ] \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N y_n \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \right] \right ) \right )\right ) \propto \exp \left ( -\frac{1}{2} \left ( \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} B^{-1}_{L+1, d} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T - 2 \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} B^{-1}_{L+1, d} m^T_{L+1, d} \right )\right ),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}B^{-1}_{L+1,d}= \bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} +\E \left [(\eta_{L+1,d})^{-2} \right ] \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L}^T \right], m^T_{L+1,d} = B_{L+1,d}\E \left [(\eta_{L+1,d})^{-2} \right ] \left ( \sum_{n}^N y_n \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \right] \right ).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-052: \begin{equation}q(b_{L+1,d}, \bW_{L+1,d}) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d} \bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1,d}^T -\frac {1}{2}\E \left [(\eta_{L+1,d})^{-2} \right ] \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\left ( y_{n,d} - \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L } \right )^{2} \right] \right ) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T -\frac {\E \left [\eta_{L+1,d}^{-2} \right ] }{2}\left ( \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L}^T \right] \right ) \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T - 2 \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N y_n \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \right] \right ) \right )\right ) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \left (\widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left (\bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} +\E \left [\eta_{L+1,d}^{-2} \right ] \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L}^T \right] \right ) \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T - 2 \E \left [\eta_{L+1,d}^{-2} \right ] \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N y_n \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \right] \right ) \right )\right ) \propto \exp \left ( -\frac{1}{2} \left ( \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} B^{-1}_{L+1, d} \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d}^T - 2 \widetilde{\bW}_{L+1, d} B^{-1}_{L+1, d} m^T_{L+1, d} \right )\right ),\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}B^{-1}_{L+1,d}= \bD^{-1}_{L+1,d} +\E \left [(\eta_{L+1,d})^{-2} \right ] \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, L}^T \right], m^T_{L+1,d} = B_{L+1,d}\E \left [(\eta_{L+1,d})^{-2} \right ] \left ( \sum_{n}^N y_n \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, L} \right] \right ).\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-223: We introduce the matrices \begin{equation}\bD_{l,d}^{-1}= \diag \left( s_0^{-2}, \E \left [\tau_l^{-1} \right ] \E \left [\psi_{l,d,1}^{-1}\right ], \ldots, \E \left [\tau_l^{-1} \right ] \E \left [ \psi_{l,d,D_{l-1}^{-1}} \right ]\right),\end{equation} and consider the terms relevant to derive each $q( b_{l,d , \bW_{l,d )$ separately: \begin{equation}q( b_{l,d}, \bW_{l,d})\propto \exp \left( - \frac{ 1}{2}\right) \right ) \right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-056: \begin{equation}q( b_{l,d}, \bW_{l,d})\propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \bD^{-1}_{l,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T - \frac{1}{2T^2} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\omega_{n,l,d}\right ] \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}^T \right ] \right ) \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T \right . \left. -\frac {\E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ]}{2} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \left ( \sum_{n}^N \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d}^2\right ] \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}^T \right ] \right ) \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T +\E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ]\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \left (\sum_{n}^N \E \left [ \gamma_{n,l,d}\right ] \E \left [ a_{n,l,d} \ba_{n,l-1}\right ] \right ) \right . + \left. \frac{1}{T} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \left (\sum_{n}^N \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} \right ] \right )- \frac{ 1}{2T} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \left (\sum_{n}^N \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\right ]\right ) \right ) \propto \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}\left (\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} B_{l,d}^{-1} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T -2 \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}B^{-1}_{l,d}m^{T}_{l,d} \right )\right ),\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-057: \begin{equation}B_{l,d}^{-1} = \bD^{-1}_{l,d}+\sum_{n}^N \left (\left ( \frac{1}{T^2} \E \left [\omega_{n,l,d}\right ] + \E \left [ (\eta_{l,d})^{-2}\right ]\E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d}\right ] \right) \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}^T \right ] \right ), m_{l,d}^T = B_{l,d}\left (\sum_{n}^N \left (\E \left [ (\eta_{l,d})^{-2} \right ] \E \left [ \gamma_{n,l,d}\right ] \E \left [ a_{n,l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n,l-1}\right ] + \frac{1}{T} \E \left [\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} \right ] \left ( \E \left [\gamma_{n,l,d} \right ] - \frac{ 1}{2}\right) \right ) \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-224: Again, completing the square, we obtain the Gaussian variational posterior \begin{equation}q( b_{l,d}, \bW_{l,d}) = \Norm \left ((b_{l,d}, \bW_{l,d}) \mid m_{l,d}, B_{l,d}\right ).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-225: The variational posterior of the augmented variables is \begin{equation}q(\bomega) \propto \exp \left ( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp \left (-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2}\right ) p(\omega_{n,l,d}) \right] \right ) \propto \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp \left ( \E \left [-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d} z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2} \right] \right ) p(\omega_{n,l,d}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-226: Thus, they are independent across width, depth, and observations, with \begin{equation}q(\omega_{n,l,d}) =\PG(\omega_{n,l,d}\mid 1, \frac{1}{T}\sqrt{ \E \left [z_{n,l,d}^2 \right ]} ) = \PG( \omega_{n,l,d} \mid 1, A_{n,l,d}),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}A_{n,l,d} = \frac{1}{T} \sqrt{\left ( \Tr \left ( \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n,l-1} \widetilde{\ba}_{n,l-1}^T\right ] \right ) \right )}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-227: The variational posterior of the binary activations is: \begin{equation}q(\bgamma) \propto \exp \left ( \E \left [\log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bSigma_{l} \right) + \log \left (\prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L\prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp(\frac{\gamma_{n,l,d} z_{n,l,d}}{T }) \right ) \right ] \right ) \propto \prod_{n}^N\prod_{l}^L \prod_{l}^{D_l}\exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2\eta_{l,d}^{2} } \E \left [\left ( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d} \left (\bW_{l,d} \ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d} \right) \right )^2 \right ] + \E \left[\frac{\gamma_{n,l,d} (\bW_{l,d} \ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d})}{T }\right ] \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-228: Therefore, the variational posterior $q(\bgamma)$ factories across observations $n = 1, \ldots, N$, layers $l = 1, \ldots, L,$ and dimensions of the layer $ d = 1, \ldots, D_l$, with each factor $q(\gamma_{n,l,d )$ given by: { { \begin{equation}q(\gamma_{n,l,d}) \propto \exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2} \E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ] \left( \gamma_{n,l,d}^2 \E \left[ \left (\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} \right )^2 \right ] - 2 \gamma_{n,l,d}\E \left [a_{n,l,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} \right ] \right ) + \frac{1}{T} \gamma_{n,l,d} \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\right ] \right) \propto \exp \left ( \gamma_{n,l,d} \left ( -\frac {\E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ]}{2} \Tr \left (\E \left[\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}^T \right ] \right ) + \E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} a_{n,l,d} \right ] + \frac{1}{T} \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\right ] \right ) \right ) \propto \exp \left (\gamma_{n,l,d} \sigma^{-1}\left(\rho_{n,l,d} \right) \right),\end{equation} where $\sigma$ is the logistic function and { {\begin{equation}\rho_{n,l,d} = \sigma \left( \E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ] \left(-\frac {1}{2} \Tr \left (\E \left[\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}^T \right ] \right ) + \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} a_{n,l,d} \right ] \right) + \frac{1}{T} \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\right ] \right).\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}q(\gamma_{n,l,d}) \propto \exp \left ( -\frac {1}{2} \E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ] \left( \gamma_{n,l,d}^2 \E \left[ \left (\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} \right )^2 \right ] - 2 \gamma_{n,l,d}\E \left [a_{n,l,d} \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} \right ] \right ) + \frac{1}{T} \gamma_{n,l,d} \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\right ] \right) \propto \exp \left ( \gamma_{n,l,d} \left ( -\frac {\E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ]}{2} \Tr \left (\E \left[\widetilde{\bW}_{l,d}^T \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}^T \right ] \right ) + \E \left [ \eta_{l,d}^{-2} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1} a_{n,l,d} \right ] + \frac{1}{T} \E \left [ \widetilde{\bW}_{l,d} \right ] \E \left [ \widetilde{\ba}_{n, l-1}\right ] \right ) \right ) \propto \exp \left (\gamma_{n,l,d} \sigma^{-1}\left(\rho_{n,l,d} \right) \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-229: Then noticing that $\sigma^{-1 (\rho) = \log(\rho(1-\rho)^{-1 )$ and combining separate factors of the variational posterior of the binary activations, we obtain: \begin{equation}q(\bgamma) \propto \prod_{n}^N\prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l}\rho_{n,l,d}^{\gamma_{n,l,d}}\left(1-\rho_{n,l,d}\right)^{1-\gamma_{n,l,d}} \propto \prod_{n}^N\prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l} \Bern \left (\gamma_{n,l,d} \mid\rho_{n,l,d} \right ).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-230: The variational posterior of the stochastic activation is \begin{equation}q(\ba) \propto \exp \left ( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bmeta_{L+1}\right) + \log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bmeta_{l} \right) \right. \right. + \left. \left.\log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp(\frac{(\gamma_{n,l,d} - \frac{1}{2}) z_{n,l,d}}{T }) \exp(-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2}) \right ] \right ) \propto \prod_{n}^N \exp \left (-\frac {1}{2} \sum_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \E \left [ \frac{1}{\eta_{L+1,d}^2} \right ] \E \left [ \left ( y_{n,d} - \bW_{L+1,d} \ba_{n, L} - b_{L+1,d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \times \prod_{n}^N\exp \left (-\frac {1}{2} \sum_{l}^L \sum_{d}^{D_l} \E \left [\frac{1}{\eta_{l,d}^2} \right ] \E \left[\left ( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d}\left (\bW_{l,d} \ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d} \right ) \right )^{2} \right] \right) \times \prod_{n}^N \exp \left( \sum_{l}^L\sum_{d}^{D_l} \E \left [ \frac{\left (\gamma_{n,l,d} - \frac{1}{2}\right ) z_{n,l,d}}{T}-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2} \right ] \right ).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-066: \begin{equation}q(\ba) \propto \exp \left ( \E \left [ \log \prod_{n}^N \Norm\left(\by_n \mid \bz_{n,L+1}, \bmeta_{L+1}\right) + \log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \Norm \left( \ba_{n,l} \mid \bgamma_{n,l} \odot \bz_{n,l} , \bmeta_{l} \right) \right. \right. + \left. \left.\log \prod_{n}^N \prod_{l}^L \prod_{d}^{D_l} \exp(\frac{(\gamma_{n,l,d} - \frac{1}{2}) z_{n,l,d}}{T }) \exp(-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2}) \right ] \right ) \propto \prod_{n}^N \exp \left (-\frac {1}{2} \sum_{d}^{D_{L+1}} \E \left [ \frac{1}{\eta_{L+1,d}^2} \right ] \E \left [ \left ( y_{n,d} - \bW_{L+1,d} \ba_{n, L} - b_{L+1,d} \right )^{2} \right] \right) \times \prod_{n}^N\exp \left (-\frac {1}{2} \sum_{l}^L \sum_{d}^{D_l} \E \left [\frac{1}{\eta_{l,d}^2} \right ] \E \left[\left ( a_{n,l,d} - \gamma_{n,l,d}\left (\bW_{l,d} \ba_{n,l-1} + b_{l,d} \right ) \right )^{2} \right] \right) \times \prod_{n}^N \exp \left( \sum_{l}^L\sum_{d}^{D_l} \E \left [ \frac{\left (\gamma_{n,l,d} - \frac{1}{2}\right ) z_{n,l,d}}{T}-\frac{\omega_{n,l,d}z_{n,l,d}^2}{2T^2} \right ] \right ).\end{equation}
